title: No Place to Lay His Head
subtitle: The scribe who came to Christ for gain, and the cost of following

stillness: Be still, and let the crowds fall away.
For the many crowds are the many cares that trouble the soul from every side.
Withdraw from them now, into a secret place, and be quiet.
gospel: Seeing the crowds about him, Jesus commanded to go over the water,
to a secret place, away from the crowds.
And on the way a certain scribe came to him and said:
Master, I will follow thee whithersoever thou shalt go.
The foxes have holes, and the birds of the air nests;
but the Son of man hath not where to lay his head.
And he said to another: Follow me.
But he answered: Lord, suffer me first to go and to bury my father.
And Jesus said to him: Follow me,
and let the dead bury their dead.
behold: Mark how he draws his disciples away across the water, to a secret place, sequestering them from the crowds:
teaching the preacher of the Gospel to shun all applause of the people, and to do nothing for show.
See, the many crowds are the many cares, that trouble the soul from every side — therefore it says, about him.
For as the smith, when he would let blood from a horse, covers its eyes, and then strikes where he will:
so the devil, willing to strike a man, covers his eyes with cares and anxieties, and then wounds him through sin, and draws out the blood of his virtues.
And on the way there comes one drawing near bodily, not spiritually — a scribe, a man learned in the law.
He says not Lord, but Master; for he comes for the cause of learning, not of serving;
not seeking a Master, but seeking gain from the Master.
Behold, as Chrysostom says, how the Lord shows by works the poverty he had taught:
he had no table, no candlestick, no house, nor any such thing.
A little dwelling indeed he had — the womb of the Virgin; a resting-place — a manger, and not his own; and the gibbet of the cross;
a sepulchre he had, but another's.
consider: This scribe willed to follow Christ for a twofold cause: for greed and temporal gain, and for boasting and vain glory.
Moved by the multitude and greatness of the signs he had seen, he wished to learn to work signs — for the gain to be acquired, or the vain glory to be won.
He sought from the Lord that same thing which Simon Magus willed to buy from Peter.
But Jesus, seeing his mind, answered not to his word only, but to his mind.
By the foxes is signified craftiness and pretence; by the birds, empty boasting and pride.
As if the Lord should say: Craft and pride lie hidden in thy heart, for thou willest to follow me for greed and vain glory;
and therefore I receive thee not into my fellowship.
For the head of Christ is God, and God dwells in the humble, and the simple, and him that trembles at his words.
In three things is this man reproved: in craftiness, for with a feigned and not a simple mind he willed to follow;
in greed, for he meant to follow him for profit; and in pride, for he longed to cling to him for empty glory.
Even now the ambitious and the simoniacs in the Church imitate him, who long to be lifted up on high,
seeking the things that are their own, not the things that are Jesus Christ's; not to minister, but to be ministered unto.
Here too are signified those seeking to enter religion, or a monastery of the rich — not out of devotion,
but that from poor men they may become rich, and from contemptible, honoured.
To such it is said: The foxes have holes, and the birds of the air nests — that is, the crafty and the proud demons have a dwelling in you, but Christ has not.
And to another, whose father he knew already dead, he said: Follow me.
But he answered: Lord, suffer me first to go and bury my father — which is a work of mercy.
Rabanus says: he did not spurn discipleship, but, the duty of his father's burial first fulfilled, he desired to follow more freely.
Yet the Lord, with a mind to correct him, says: Follow me, notwithstanding thy father's death.
It is not of the order of charity to set the lesser good before the greater.
Let the dead — dead by the death of guilt — bury their dead, dead by the death of nature.
The unbelieving are called dead, because they lack faith, which is the life of the soul: The just man lives by faith.
Here is taken away the carnal affection of kinsmen; carnal affection must be wholly left behind by those who follow Christ.
And this is an argument against those who excuse themselves, pleading the care of their parents; and against those who delay.
For if he would not be held back for a burial, much less for a long delay.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, good Master, searcher of hearts, and knower of secrets,
shut out and renew from me all craftiness and pretence,
and make me thy true and faithful disciple,
and without feigning to follow thee, the truthful Master.
Grant also that I may not put off following thee for any carnal affection,
nor prefer the lesser good to the greater.
Grant me, Lord my God, that putting my hand to the plough of penance,
and purposing to follow thee through a state of better life,
I may not look back — in deed or in purpose — by returning to my former state,
lest I be made unfit for the obtaining of the kingdom of God. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Vulpes foveas habent, ad quiescendum et latitandum, et volucres cœli nidos habent ad quos ascendunt et confugiunt, Filius autem hominis non habet ubi caput suum reclinet.
latinJewel.english: The foxes have holes, to rest and to hide in, and the birds of the air have nests to which they ascend and flee; but the Son of man hath not where to lay his head.
